en.extract:
The black-lored babbler or Sharpe's pied-babbler (Turdoides sharpei) is a species of bird in the family Leiothrichidae.  It is found in southwestern Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, Burundi, Rwanda, and the part of the Democratic Republic of the Congo immediately adjacent to the three last-named countries.   This bird was formerly considered the same species as Turdoides melanops of southern Africa, now known as the black-faced babbler.
These birds are mostly grey-brown with white mottling, especially on the underparts, that varies according to location and the individual. The population near Nanyuki, Kenya, is darker but can have a pure white chin or entire throat.  The combination of pale yellow or white eyes and black lores (the areas between the eye and the bill) separates adults of this species from similar babblers except melanops, though all juvenile babblers have brown eyes.
In Kenya, single birds give repeated single or double harsh notes such as waaach or a muffled kurr-ack; pairs or groups give longer phrases in chorus.  The tempo is frequently slow for a babbler.  They are most vocal in the early morning and late afternoon.
Like other Turdoides, it is found low or on the ground in or near dense woody vegetation, including in cultivated areas.  Kenyan birds forage in bushes and tall grass.  They are "restless, noisy, and suspicious" and "typical gregarious babblers".
Single birds give repeated single or double harsh notes such as waaach or a muffled kurr-ack; pairs or groups give longer phrases in chorus.  The tempo is frequently slow for a babbler.  They are most vocal in the early morning and late afternoon.


References
 
Collar, N. J. & Robson, C. 2007. Family Timaliidae (Babblers)  pp. 70 – 291 in; del Hoyo, J., Elliott, A. & Christie, D.A. eds. Handbook of the Birds of the World, Vol. 12. Picathartes to Tits and Chickadees. Lynx Edicions, Barcelona.

The '''black-lored babbler''' or '''Sharpe's pied-babbler''' ('''''Turdoides sharpei''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Leiothrichidae]].  It is found in southwestern [[Kenya]],<ref name=ZTP>{{cite book |author1=Zimmerman, Dale A. |author2=Turner, Donald A. |author3=Pearson, David J. |name-list-style=amp | year = 1999 | title = Birds of Kenya and Northern Tanzania, Field Guide Edition | publisher = Princeton University Press | pages = 196–197, 435 | isbn = 0-691-01022-6 }}</ref> [[Tanzania]], [[Uganda]], [[Burundi]], [[Rwanda]], and the part of the [[Democratic Republic of the Congo]] immediately adjacent<ref name=TP>{{cite web |last=Pedersen |first=Tommy |year=2001 |title=Democratic Republic of CONGO - A bird checklist |url=http://www.birdlist.org/downloads/Congo-acompletechecklist.zip |access-date=3 August 2007 |format=zip |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060225233145/http://birdlist.org/downloads/Congo-acompletechecklist.zip |archive-date=25 February 2006 }} See also [http://www.tommypedersen.com/DRC.htm this site]  {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070928231030/http://www.tommypedersen.com/DRC.htm |date=September 28, 2007  }} where one can request an updated checklist.</ref> to the three last-named countries.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" />   This bird was formerly considered the same species as ''[[Turdoides melanops]]'' of southern Africa, now known as the black-faced babbler.<ref name=ZTP/>

These birds are mostly grey-brown with white mottling, especially on the underparts, that varies according to location and the individual. The population near [[Nanyuki]], Kenya, is darker but can have a pure white chin or entire throat.  The combination of pale yellow or white eyes and black lores (the areas between the eye and the bill) separates adults of this species from similar babblers except ''melanops'', though all juvenile babblers have brown eyes.<ref name=ZTP/>

In Kenya, single birds give repeated single or double harsh notes such as ''waaach'' or a muffled ''kurr-ack''; pairs or groups give longer phrases in chorus.  The tempo is frequently slow for a babbler.<ref name = ZTP/>  They are most vocal in the early morning and late afternoon.<ref name=ZTP0>{{cite book |author1=Zimmerman, Dale A. |author2=Turner, Donald A. |author3=Pearson, David J. |name-list-style=amp | year = 1996 | title = Birds of Kenya and Northern Tanzania | publisher = Princeton University Press | pages = 196–197, 435 | isbn = 0-691-02658-0 }}</ref>

Like other ''Turdoides'', it is found low or on the ground in or near dense woody vegetation, including in cultivated areas.  Kenyan birds forage in bushes and tall grass.  They are "restless, noisy, and suspicious"<ref name=ZTP0/> and "typical gregarious babblers".<ref name=ZTP/>

Single birds give repeated single or double harsh notes such as ''waaach'' or a muffled ''kurr-ack''; pairs or groups give longer phrases in chorus.  The tempo is frequently slow for a babbler.<ref name = ZTP/>  They are most vocal in the early morning and late afternoon.<ref name=ZTP0/>

zh.extract:
黑眼先鸫鹛（学名：Turdoides sharpei），是雀形目噪鹛科的一种鸟类。
黑眼先鸫鹛体重约79.3克，翼长约112.2毫米，嘴峰长约23毫米，喙宽度约4.4毫米，喙厚度约6.4毫米，跗蹠长约36.2毫米，尾长约108.7毫米。黑眼先鸫鹛在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在半开放的中等高度的林地中，食性为肉食性，主要食物来源是陆生无脊椎动物。


亚种
T. s. sharpei：分布于刚果河东岸至肯尼亚西部和坦桑尼亚西部。
T. s. vepres：分布于肯尼亚中部。
